summary: Joel Spolsky recaps Stack Overflow's 2010 growth, highlighting the company's expansion from 7 million to 16.6 million monthly visitors, raising $6 million in venture capital, and growing from 3 to 27 employees. He emphasizes the launch of Stack Exchange as a network of 33 Q&A sites beyond programming, and touts the platform's high answer rates as its key differentiator. The post also covers the company's new New York office and development team accomplishments. It's essentially a celebratory year-in-review that doubles as a vision statement for Stack Exchange's expansion into new subject areas.
key_points: Stack Overflow grew 131% in traffic during 2010, reaching 16.6 million monthly unique visitors and 72.8 million monthly page views
The key quality metric is percentage of questions answered (over 80%), which Spolsky considers more important than raw traffic numbers
Stack Exchange launched as a democratically-driven network of Q&A sites beyond programming, growing to 33 sites with 1.5 million monthly visitors in six months
The Area 51 process ensures new sites only launch when they have critical mass of knowledgeable users, and underperforming sites get shut down
The company scaled from 3 remote developers to 27 employees with a physical office, funded by $6 million in VC from Union Square Ventures
money_quotes: The expert Q&A model that Stack Overflow pioneered is really working.
The true measure of success for any Internet company is how often people come up to me in swank hotel lobbies and offer to buy me meals, let me use their corporate jet, etc.
When was the last time you met a programmer who didn't use 'El Stack'?
All this TRAFFIC isn't worth a thing if people aren't getting answers to their questions.
We learned a long time ago that the only way to get questions answered promptly is to have a critical mass of knowledgeable users.
If a proposed site doesn't have critical mass, we just won't create it. Even if it does get created, it has to maintain a certain level of traffic and quality or we'll close it down.
Does your company have a Star Trek Couch? I didn't think so.
key_insight: Stack Overflow's real competitive advantage isn't traffic but its answer rate — the expert Q&A model works because it ensures critical mass of knowledgeable users before launching any new community.
